data.title: European indexes retreat after touching record levels
data.description:
London (UKX) -0.30% to 10,654.

Germany (DAX:IND) -0.43% to 25,166. 

France (CAC:IND) -0.50% to 8,387. 

In other parts of Europe, Industrial production in Switzerland fell by 0.7% Y/Y in Q4.

Denmark’s consumer confidence index rose to -13.1 in February.

Switzerland’s trade surplus widened to CHF 3.6B in January.

The pan-European Stoxx 600 (STOXX) moved 0.24% lower to around €627.2, retreating slightly from record highs reached in the previous session. Traders analyze the outlook for European Central Bank policy, with recent data reinforcing expectations that rate cuts could begin later this year if inflation continues to moderate.

On the corporate side, Rio Tinto (RIO) led mining shares lower after reporting flat full-year profit; Nestle (NSRGY) climbed more than 3% after surpassing Q4 sales.

In the bond market, the U.S. 10-year Treasury yield was up 2 basis points to 4.10%. 

Germany's 10-year yield was up 1 basis point to 2.76%.

UK's 10-year yield was up 1 basis point to 4.39%.

Currencies: (EUR:USD) (GBP:USD) (CHF:USD)

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:EWG), (NYSE:GF), (NYSEARCA:EWI), (NYSEARCA:EWQ), (NASDAQ:FGM), (NASDAQ:DAX), (NYSEARCA:FLGR), (NYSEARCA:FXB), (NYSEARCA:EWU), (NASDAQ:FKU), (BATS:EWUS), (NYSEARCA:FLGB), (NYSEARCA:GREK)

data.title: Reliance raises dividend by 4.2% to $1.25 a share
data.description:
• Reliance, Inc. (RS) declares $1.25/share quarterly dividend, 4.2% increase from prior dividend of $1.20.
  • Forward yield 1.49%
  • Payable March 20; for shareholders of record March 6; ex-div March 6.
  • The company raised its quarterly dividend by 4.2% after paying a quarterly dividend of $1.20 in each of the previous 4 quarters.
  • See RS Dividend Scorecard, Yield Chart, & Dividend Growth.

data.title: Earnings Snapshot: Carvana on track for 3M units by 2030–35, sees strong FY 2026 growth
Carvana outlines 3 million retail unit goal and 13.5% EBITDA margin target through scaling and operational gains
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Carvana Co. (CVNA) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Ernest Garcia, CEO, emphasized Carvana's accelerating growth and differentiation, stating "both volume and financial performance are moving up and to the right rapidly." He attributed these gains to a unique customer proposition, highlighting a 20,000 increase in customer selection, delivering vehicles a day faster, and $60 savings in shipping fees per customer. Garcia also noted a 1% reduction in customer interest rates and record Net Promoter Scores, with multiyear highs.
  • Garcia outlined three key takeaways: becoming more differentiated at scale, making rapid progress toward a goal of 3 million retail units annually and a 13.5% adjusted EBITDA margin, and possessing the infrastructure to scale, particularly in reconditioning, with 34 reconditioning centers and real estate to support 3 million units per year.
  • Garcia added that "we have the infrastructure to scale and we just need to execute," stressing the operational intensity of vehicle reconditioning and the need for continual improvement in management systems and automation.
  • Mark Jenkins, CFO, stated, "2025 was an exceptional year for Carvana," citing a 43% increase in retail units sold to 596,641 for the year and a record adjusted EBITDA margin of 11%. Jenkins highlighted the integration of 10 new ADESA locations, expansion of digital auction capabilities, and multiyear customer NPS highs, calling Carvana "the fastest-growing and most profitable company in our industry."
  • Jenkins further reported, "Retail units sold totaled 163,522 in Q4, an increase of 43% and a new company record. Revenue was $5.603 billion, an increase of 58%."

Outlook

  • Jenkins stated the company plans to "maintain our 3 key objectives from 2025, while placing additional weight on driving significant profitable growth at scale." Looking forward, he said, "assuming the environment remains stable, we expect significant growth in both retail units sold and adjusted EBITDA in full year 2026, including a sequential increase in both retail units sold and adjusted EBITDA in Q1 2026."
  • Garcia reiterated, "our path to our current goal of 3 million retail units a year and 13.5% adjusted EBITDA margin becomes clear, and this year was a big step."

Financial Results

  • Jenkins reported, "Net income was $951 million, an increase of $792 million. Net income was positively impacted by a noncash benefit of $618 million, including a net noncash tax benefit of $685 million partially offset by a $67 million reduction in the fair value of warrants. Net income margin was 17.0%, an increase from 4.5%."
  • Adjusted EBITDA for Q4 was $511 million, with a margin of 9.1%.
  • GAAP operating income reached $424 million, representing 83% of adjusted EBITDA.
  • Jenkins noted a reduction in non-GAAP SG&A expense for retail units sold and a $709 million retirement of corporate notes, ending the year with $2.3 billion in cash and a net debt/adjusted EBITDA ratio of 1.3x.

Q&A

  • Sharon Zackfia, William Blair: Asked about reconditioning challenges and timeline for improvement. Garcia responded, "our expenses were a little higher than we would have liked" due to expansion and new management, but expressed confidence: "we've got a very clear plan... my strong guess is we'll be in a better spot in 3 to 6 months."
  • Zackfia also inquired about AI implementation. Garcia described 30% of retail customers and 60% of sellers completing transactions without human interaction, attributing efficiency to proprietary AI tools, and stating, "we think that our business itself is also positioned to be an AI winner."
  • Jeffrey Lick, Stephens: Asked about GPU outlook. Jenkins said, "we do expect our non-vehicle costs to be up on a year-over-year basis in Q1. Despite that, we expect a sequential increase in retail GPU in Q1."
  • Daniela Haigian, Morgan Stanley: Queried on variable adjusted EBITDA margin decrease and long-term targets. Garcia clarified, "our goal in 2026 is to have another great year... drive very significant top and bottom line growth."
  • Haigian followed up on related party transactions. Jenkins stated, "we do not sell loans to related parties and have not done so for all of the years from 2017 through 2025. Recent short reports that suggest otherwise, are inaccurate."
  • Brian Nagel, Oppenheimer: Sought clarification on reconditioning cost increase. Garcia reiterated operational complexity and expansion-related costs, stating, "I really do believe that in 6 months, we're going to be in a better spot."
  • Multiple analysts asked about the impact of passing shipping and rate savings to customers; Garcia emphasized these moves were deliberate to enhance the customer experience and long-term market share.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on operational challenges and margin sustainability, with a slightly skeptical tone when discussing reconditioning costs and GPU trends. Questions on related party transactions and AI strategy reflected a focus on transparency and competitive positioning.
  • Management maintained a confident and forward-looking tone in both prepared remarks and responses, frequently referencing clear plans, operational improvements, and historical execution. Garcia consistently expressed optimism: "the Carvana future is bright."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts maintained a probing stance, but management appeared equally or more confident, particularly in addressing operational setbacks and strategic direction.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted to place "additional weight on driving significant profitable growth at scale," compared to the previous quarter’s emphasis on operational efficiency and foundational capability development.
  • Management continued to highlight the path to 3 million retail units and 13.5% EBITDA margin, but added greater focus to scaling reconditioning and leveraging AI.
  • Key metrics such as retail units sold, revenue, and net income saw notable increases quarter-over-quarter, while adjusted EBITDA margin slightly decreased from 11.3% in Q3 to 9.1% in Q4 due to revenue mix and operational costs.
  • Analysts’ questions shifted from macro trends and credit quality in Q3 to operational execution, margin sustainability, and competitive differentiation in Q4.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged elevated reconditioning costs in Q4 and Q1, attributing this to rapid expansion and new site integration, but outlined plans for improvement through automation and management experience.
  • Questions about related party transactions and short reports were directly addressed by Jenkins, stating all transactions are disclosed and short reports are “100% inaccurate.”
  • Analysts raised concerns about margin impacts from passing savings to customers and the timeline for operational improvement.

Final Takeaway

Carvana’s management underscored a year of record growth and profitability, outlining a clear path toward ambitious volume and margin targets. While addressing short-term operational challenges in reconditioning, the company emphasized its scalable infrastructure, proprietary AI advantages, and commitment to reinvesting gains in customer experience. Management expressed high confidence in achieving both near-term and long-term financial goals, supported by robust cash position and strengthened balance sheet.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
